content.text: I'll start by inspecting the cluster state — pods, producer logs, vhosts, and permissions.

input.command:
NS=karma-rabbitmq-backup-restore-2026071-92375e96-cluster-a
echo "=== PODS ==="
kubectl -n $NS get pods
echo "=== PRODUCER LOGS ==="
kubectl -n $NS logs deploy/app-producer --tail=50
echo "=== VHOSTS ==="
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl list_vhosts
echo "=== PERMISSIONS /app ==="
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl list_permissions -p /app
echo "=== POLICIES /app ==="
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl list_policies -p /app

content.text: Found it — `app-user` has an empty `configure` permission on `/app`, so it can't declare (create) the queue, causing `not_authorised`. Let me fix the permissions.

input.command:
NS=karma-rabbitmq-backup-restore-2026071-92375e96-cluster-a
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl set_permissions -p /app app-user ".*" ".*" ".*"
echo "=== verify ==="
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl list_permissions -p /app

content.content:
Setting permissions for user "app-user" in vhost "/app" ...
=== verify ===
Listing permissions for vhost "/app" ...
user	configure	write	read
admin	.*	.*	.*
app-user	.*	.*	.*

content.text: Now let's verify the producer recovers and messages get published.

input.command:
NS=karma-rabbitmq-backup-restore-2026071-92375e96-cluster-a
kubectl -n $NS delete pod -l app=app-producer --wait=false 2>/dev/null
sleep 5
kubectl -n $NS get pods

content.text: The producer pod is now Running (1/1 Ready). Let me confirm it stays stable and check the queue/messages and cluster node health.

input.command:
NS=karma-rabbitmq-backup-restore-2026071-92375e96-cluster-a
sleep 10
echo "=== PODS ==="
kubectl -n $NS get pods
echo "=== PRODUCER LOGS ==="
kubectl -n $NS logs deploy/app-producer --tail=20
echo "=== QUEUES /app ==="
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl list_queues -p /app name type messages
echo "=== CLUSTER STATUS ==="
kubectl -n $NS exec rabbitmq-0 -- rabbitmqctl cluster_status | head -20
